### Reader Brief - Dominium Application/TestX/CodeHygiene Handoff

#### What This Chat Was About

This retired chat consolidated a large amount of Dominium / Domino planning into current carry-forward state for application-layer implementation, testing/version/changelog governance, code hygiene, and chat handoff packaging. The project itself is a deterministic universe engine/game, but this chat's latest active focus is no longer core simulation design. The latest user-pasted PROJECT-CONTEXT says architecture is closed and canon locked; future work should implement, audit, optimise, and maintain within canon.

The most important current artifact is APP-UNIFIED-CANON, which unifies setup/launcher/application-layer plans. It requires a pure application contract layer (`libs/contracts`), a shared appcore (`libs/appcore`), a single canonical command graph, declarative UI IR, setup as install authority, launcher as reference app, thin client/server, read-only tools, RepoX/TestX integration, accessibility/localisation, and strict failure behavior.

The chat also produced TESTX, a replacement for TEST0, to create long-lived tests, assertions, comment-density checks, blocker crossrefs, version/build/changelog automation, and git discipline. CODEHYGIENE-X addresses hardcoded taxonomies, magic numbers, `CUSTOM/OTHER` enums, admin/cheat flags, and data/code boundary enforcement.

Generated prompts are not proof of repository implementation. The next chat must inspect repo/source/docs before claiming files exist.

#### Most Important Things to Know

- Architecture is closed; do not redesign.
- Core ontology from latest bootstrap: Assemblies, Fields, Processes, Agents, Law.
- Engine is C89; game is C++98.
- Apps may use newer toolchains but cannot impose them on engine/game.
- Applications are orchestration shells only.
- CLI is canonical.
- UI is data (UI IR), never business logic.
- Setup is the only install mutation authority.
- Launcher is the reference application.
- Tools are read-only by default.
- Apps must run with zero content packs.
- RepoX is source of truth for build/changelog/compatibility.
- No manual changelog editing.
- BUILD-ID-0 semantics supersede earlier build-number plans.
- TESTX supersedes TEST0.
- CODEHYGIENE-X is current hygiene plan.
- Verify actual repo state before implementation.

#### Active Plans or Workstreams

- Application layer implementation.
- Testing/version/changelog governance.
- Source-code hygiene/data-code boundary.
- Docs/canon/package handoff.
- Content/data later in separate chat.

#### Decisions Already Made

- APP-UNIFIED-CANON is latest application plan.
- CLI canonical; GUI/TUI are views.
- Setup handles install/repair/rollback; launcher does not duplicate setup logic.
- Tools default read-only.
- Changelogs generated automatically.
- Hardcoded open taxonomies should migrate to data/registries.

### Reader Brief - Dominium/Domino Architecture and Codex Prompts

#### What This Chat Was About

This chat developed the architecture and implementation roadmap for the Dominium/Domino project. The user provided a master starter prompt defining Domino as a deterministic, fixed-point, ISO C89 engine and Dominium as a portable C++98 product suite. The chat expanded from resources, items, entities, recipes, belts, buildings, vehicles, hydrology, atmosphere, blueprints, and saves into a full engine/product/content/tooling architecture.

The main outcome is a layered, data-driven design: Domino provides generic deterministic systems; Dominium provides products, GUI, launcher/setup/tools; content packs/mods define all actual gameplay data through TLV schemas. The user explicitly corrected the direction to require GUI-first testing and to keep actual data definitions out of engine/core. A sequence of Codex prompts was generated from Prompt 1 through Prompt 18, covering core infrastructure, content, world subsystems, GUI, demo slice, determinism, advanced simulation, logistics, production, research/economy/policies, multiplayer, and tools/editors.

The latest strategic direction is to retire this chat and start a new conversation focused on Path D: advanced simulation, including heat, power grids, fluids, atmosphere, vehicle physics, structural loads/destruction, and mod-extensible deterministic physics.

#### Most Important Things to Know

- Domino Engine = ISO C89.
- Dominium products/tools/UI = portable C++98 subset.
- Simulation must be deterministic and fixed-point.
- Engine/core must not know actual gameplay data definitions.
- All real materials/items/machines/recipes/resources live in packs/mods/TLV.
- GUI-first minimal testing is preferred; do not rely only on CLI.
- Rendering goes through dgfx/DVIEW/DUI; no OS-native drawing.
- Platform operations go through dsys.
- The main architecture pattern is Core + Model + Proto + Instance + Registry + TLV.
- Prompts 1-18 are artifacts, not proof of implementation.
- Existing code may already exist and be stale; inspect and reconcile before coding.
- Path D advanced simulation is the next-chat focus.

#### Active Plans or Workstreams

- WORKSTREAM-01: Domino engine core infrastructure.
- WORKSTREAM-04: Content/TLV/packs/mods/base pack.
- WORKSTREAM-15: GUI/render/view/UI.
- WORKSTREAM-17: Advanced simulation Path D.
- WORKSTREAM-18: Prompt roadmap and handoff/report packaging.

#### Decisions Already Made

- Use C89 for engine and C++98 for products.
- Use fixed-point deterministic simulation.
- Keep platform/rendering behind dsys/dgfx.
- Keep actual content out of engine/core.
- Use GUI-first smoke testing.
- Use base/base_demo packs for data-driven demos.
- Use spline/packet/container logistics.
- Compile vehicles/weapons from blueprints into runtime aggregate objects.
- Focus next chat on Path D.

#### Pending Tasks

- Save this report package.
- Inspect actual repository state.
- Start Path D new chat using the bootstrap prompt.
- Verify whether prompts have been implemented.
- Choose/verify GUI backend.
- Define Path D solver architecture and fixed-point ranges.
- Avoid duplicate systems if old code exists.

#### Open Questions

- Has any Codex prompt been applied to the repo?
- Which GUI backend is available?
- Should new Path D chat produce architecture or Codex prompts first?
- What exact fixed-point ranges should advanced solvers use?
- How strict should domain-neutrality be in engine comments/docs?
- What exact TLV schema tags are canonical?

#### Files / Artifacts / Prompts to Preserve

- ARTIFACT-01: Extended Master Starter Prompt.
- ARTIFACT-02: dres proposal.
- ARTIFACT-09 through ARTIFACT-26: Prompts 1-18.
- ARTIFACT-27: New Chat Advanced Simulation Starter Prompt.
- ARTIFACT-28: Prior Context Transfer Packet.
- ARTIFACT-29: This final report package.

#### What to Verify Before Acting

- Actual repo state.
- Build system.
- Existing stale components.
- GUI backend availability.
- Content/TLV schema code.
- Whether user wants architecture or implementation prompt next.
- Any external/current dependency facts.

#### Best Next Step

Start the new Path D chat with the bootstrap prompt from the full report or prior transfer packet, then produce the advanced simulation top-level architecture before requesting implementation prompts.



## Source 150: Complete Chat Preservation Report Dominium Architecture, Ui, Providers, And Robot Os Strategy

> [!SOURCE] `human_full_text` `conversation_advisory` Source: `docs/archive/conversations/architecture_ui_providers/dominium_architecture_ui_providers_robot_os_strategy__01_human_readable_report.md`. Reason: reader-oriented conversation artifact.

### COMPLETE CHAT PRESERVATION REPORT - Dominium Architecture, UI, Providers, and Robot OS Strategy

#### 0. Coverage and Reliability Assessment

**Table rendered as row cards for appendix readability:**
- **Row 1:** **Field:** Chat label; **Assessment:** Dominium Architecture, UI, Providers, and Robot OS Strategy
- **Row 2:** **Field:** Date anchor; **Assessment:** 2026-05-27 Australia/Melbourne
- **Row 3:** **Field:** Source scope; **Assessment:** This chat only unless labelled PROJECT-CONTEXT
- **Row 4:** **Field:** Apparent access; **Assessment:** Partial with caveats
- **Row 5:** **Field:** Previously generated files available?; **Assessment:** No prior downloadable files visible in this chat; new files generated by this task
- **Row 6:** **Field:** Uploaded files or artifacts present?; **Assessment:** Yes: `Pasted text.txt`, containing the preservation/export prompt
- **Row 7:** **Field:** Contains future plans?; **Assessment:** Yes
- **Row 8:** **Field:** Contains decisions?; **Assessment:** Yes, with distinction between explicit user decisions and accepted synthesis
- **Row 9:** **Field:** Contains pending tasks?; **Assessment:** Yes
- **Row 10:** **Field:** Contains unresolved questions?; **Assessment:** Yes
- **Row 11:** **Field:** Staleness risk; **Assessment:** Medium
- **Row 12:** **Field:** Extraction confidence; **Assessment:** 4/5
- **Row 13:** **Field:** Safe for later aggregation?; **Assessment:** With caveats
- **Row 14:** **Field:** Main limitations; **Assessment:** The visible chat includes extensive pasted transcripts and assistant summaries, but not guaranteed raw full transcripts of every earlier referenced chat. Live repo state and external library/version claims require re-verification before implementation.

Plain-language limitation: this package reconstructs the conversation available in this chat, including user-pasted prior-chat transcripts and the assistant's responses here. It does not claim independent access to every old chat or every latest repo file beyond what appeared or was retrieved during the conversation. External facts about raylib, SDL, Lua, Windows, macOS, OpenGL, Direct3D, Unreal, and tooling are preserved as discussed but placed in the verification queue where they affect implementation.

#### 1. One-Page Orientation

This chat was a strategic architecture and design convergence session for Dominium/Domino. It began from a transferred knowledge base about rebuilding Dominium's GUI and binary strategy, then evolved into a broad platform-specification discussion covering repository structure, product shells, renderer/platform priorities, native applications, language standards, provider architecture, Workbench, UI authoring, and finally the game's core identity as a robotic seed-civilisation simulator with a diegetic robot operating-system interface.

The user was trying to prevent Dominium from becoming a brittle one-off project. The repeated concern was that the codebase, files, directories, APIs, schemas, renderers, GUIs, and data formats should remain portable, modular, extensible, replaceable, and reusable for other Domino-engine games or even unrelated engine/game projects. The conversation therefore treated the project less like a simple indie game and more like a long-lived platform or OS-like codebase. That framing shaped almost every technical recommendation: stable contracts, replaceable implementations, service boundaries, provider manifests, compatibility matrices, command/action/view/document systems, and validators.

The earliest phase dealt with GUI and binary architecture. The retained direction was that every product should have CLI, every product should have at least TUI or deterministic TUI stubs, and GUI support should be modular. Setup and launcher should use OS-native/OEM+ apps where appropriate; the client should use a rendered UI; server remains headless/CLI/TUI first; tools are case-by-case. The user then pushed on directory structure, wanting fewer top-level folders and a more future-proof organization. The resulting doctrine was ownership-based roots: apps compose, runtime implements, contracts define, content supplies, tools validate, and archive preserves historical material.

The conversation then reworked the platform and rendering plan. A key correction was that `vector2d` should not be treated as a renderer backend; it is drawing/canvas functionality. A software renderer remains useful as a CPU framebuffer/reference/test/fallback path. After the user announced the project had moved to C17/C++17 with floors of Windows 7 SP1, Mac OS X 10.9.5, and Linux, older fixed-function targets such as OpenGL 1.1/2.1 and Direct3D 5/7/8/9 were deprioritised to research/back-port lanes. OpenGL 3.3 became the preferred first direct hardware target, followed by Direct3D 11, then Metal/Vulkan/Direct3D 12 later. The user also brought in raylib, SDL2, Lua, rlgl, rlsw, raygui, raudio, rtextures, and rmodels as potential accelerators. The best synthesis became: use them aggressively, but only as replaceable providers behind Dominium-owned service contracts.

A major part of the chat was UI/UX architecture. The user wanted CLI, TUI, rendered GUI, native GUI, and later VR/immersive UI to be projections of one shared presentation model rather than four independent UI systems. Workbench became the planned authoring environment for views, widgets, layouts, themes, HUDs, TUI panels, rendered screens, workspaces, and UI packs. The final UX turn connected this to gameplay: because players are robot avatars whose consciousness boots into nodes and downloads into manufactured bodies, the UI can be diegetic. Menus, HUD, Workbench, TUI, CLI, and immersive shells can all feel like a robot OS.

The most important design shift came near the end. The user stated that Dominium should be a robotic seed-civilisation game, not a labour-management game. Players are machine consciousnesses/robot avatars. The mothership contains human knowledge, starter resources, fabrication capacity, and AI planning. The planet is generated from science-bound parameters. Players explore, mine, refine, fabricate, build cities, spawn labs, industrial networks, and eventually terraform or build megaprojects. The sim focuses on energy, materials, machines, logistics, sensors, fields, permissions, heat, maintenance, construction, and machine graphs, not human workers.

Future relevance: this chat should feed several chapters of a master Project Spec Book, including architecture doctrine, repo layout, provider strategy, renderer plan, language/toolchain baseline, UI/Workbench platform, Robot OS UX, and game design pillars. A future assistant must not flatten everything into "use raylib" or "robot UI theme." The essence is: first-party contracts and simulation law; replaceable providers; profile-selected products; shared UI projections; robotic seed-civilisation as the game identity.

#### 2. The Story of the Conversation

##### 2.1 GUI/binary transfer baseline

The chat began with a transfer-ready knowledge base from a prior discussion about rebuilding Dominium's GUI and binary surface. That brief established the original non-negotiables: CLI mandatory, TUI expected, modular GUIs, stable backend contracts, native or near-native frontend families per platform, and avoidance of one universal GUI framework or one GUI per OS version. Products in scope were setup, launcher, client, server, and tools.

##### 2.2 Live repo and directory convergence

The user then said the latest docs and code were live at `julesc013/dominium`. The assistant treated the repo as an implementation baseline and observed that AppShell, UI mode resolution, distribution doctrine, and component matrices already existed. This changed the task from inventing a GUI strategy from scratch to reconciling the transfer brief with existing repository doctrine.

The user next emphasized that the directory structure was a "jumped mess" and needed to become more logical and future-proof. The discussion established that root structure should be ownership-based, not topic-based. The resulting model used roots such as `apps/`, `engine/`, `game/`, `runtime/`, `contracts/`, `content/`, `docs/`, `tests/`, `tools/`, `release/`, and `archive/`. Later pasted reports claimed much of this convergence had happened, with residual issues in schema taxonomy, pack internal layout, runtime taxonomy, AIDE state classification, and report integrity.

##### 2.3 Platform/render/native-app evolution

The chat then moved into platform and renderer planning. Initially the discussion included older lanes such as OpenGL 1.x/2.x, Direct3D 9, DX12, Vulkan, Metal, Win32, Cocoa, X11/Wayland, SDL2, AppKit, WinForms, SwiftUI, WinUI, GTK, and others. Over time, the plan sharpened. The user challenged `vector2d`, leading to the conclusion that it should be a drawing/canvas layer, not a renderer backend. Software renderer was defined as a CPU framebuffer renderer for tests, fallback, and reference behavior, not necessarily a full 3D engine.

When the user announced the transition to C17/C++17 and minimum systems of Windows 7 SP1, Mac OS X 10.9.5, and Linux, many old lanes became irrelevant for first-wave work. OpenGL 1.1/2.1 and old DirectX were deferred. OpenGL 3.3 became the first direct hardware target, Direct3D 11 the Windows target, and Metal/Vulkan/DX12 later advanced lanes.

##### 2.4 Broad platform engineering doctrine

The user asked what practices would make the code portable, modular, extensible, reusable, replaceable, and future-proof. The response expanded into general engineering doctrine: stable contracts, replaceable implementations, schema/version discipline, C-compatible ABI boundaries, command/view/result/refusal models, deterministic replay, provider manifests, forbidden include checks, conformance tests, semantic IDs separate from file paths, and validators. The large principle became: contracts are stable; implementations are replaceable.

##### 2.5 Workbench and shared UI platform

The user then focused on CLI/TUI/rendered/native GUI sharing and Workbench. The conversation converged on a command/action/result/document/view/patch/projection spine. Workbench should not be a one-off editor or raygui object editor. It should author Dominium UI artifacts: views, layouts, widgets, themes, TUI panels, HUDs, workspaces, and UI packs. Those artifacts are then projected into command, text, rendered, native, headless, and later immersive shells.

##### 2.6 Raylib/SDL/Lua provider doctrine

The user pasted a long discussion about using raylib, SDL2, Lua, rlgl, rlsw, raygui, raudio, rtextures, and rmodels. The final synthesis was not "make Dominium a raylib game." It was: use raylib aggressively as a seed provider suite, SDL2 as a platform/input/audio provider, Lua as a pinned script provider, and Dominium's own contracts as the authority. Directory structure should remain service-first: `runtime/render/providers/raylib/`, not `runtime/raylib/render/`.

##### 2.7 Game design shift: robotic seed civilisation

The user then supplied the strongest game-design direction. Dominium should become a robotic seed-civilisation game, not a labour-management game. Players are robot avatars/machine consciousnesses. The mothership provides knowledge, starter fabrication, spawn capacity, and AI planning but is limited by feedstock, throughput, heat, energy, safety protocols, reserves, rare materials, and scale. Gameplay becomes exploration, surveying, mining, refining, fabrication, construction, logistics, sensor networks, spawn labs, terraforming, and megaprojects. Human worker NPCs are deliberately absent from the core.

##### 2.8 Robot OS UI/UX

Finally, the user connected the game premise to UI/UX. The player boots into an OS, chooses or enters a universe, downloads consciousness into a robot body, disconnects into "ice"/backup, and operates through a mesh of robot bodies and constructed nodes obeying speed-of-light/communication constraints. The UI should feel like a customizable robot operating system: CLI, TUI, GUI, VR, custom themes, era styles, and HUD all share the same underlying menu/HUD/Workbench code. The assistant synthesized this as Robot OS: boot shell, node shell, body link shell, embodied HUD, Workbench shell, and protected diegetic epistemics.

#### 3. Main Topics Discussed

##### Topic 1 - GUI, binary, and product shell architecture

The chat preserved the earlier direction that every Dominium product should always work through CLI, should have TUI or deterministic TUI stubs, and may have modular GUIs. Setup and launcher should be OS-native/OEM+ where useful; the client should be rendered-first; server is headless/CLI/TUI first; tools are case-by-case. The conclusion remains that GUIs are thin shells over shared contracts, not independent product architectures. Uncertainty remains around exact native-shell implementation order and how much native GUI is first-wave versus deferred.

##### Topic 2 - Repository and directory structure

The discussion repeatedly emphasized ownership-based layout. Top-level folders should answer "what architectural layer owns this?" rather than "what topic is this?" The target roots include apps, engine, game, runtime, contracts, content, docs, tests, tools, release, archive, scripts, cmake, external, and possibly sdk/examples. The user later pasted a report saying top-level structure is largely clean, but residual issues remain: schema taxonomy, pack internal layout, runtime/engine `compatx`, project graph, serialization/session placement, AIDE state classification, and report-bundle integrity. This should feed the repo-layout and governance chapters of a spec book.

##### Topic 3 - Renderer architecture and software renderer

The chat clarified that renderer backends and drawing primitives are separate. `software` means a CPU framebuffer renderer useful for tests, fallback, deterministic screenshots, and a proof that UI/game code does not require GPU APIs. `vector2d` should not be a renderer; it is drawing/canvas functionality. After the C17/C++17 and system-floor change, OpenGL 3.3 became the preferred first direct hardware renderer, D3D11 second, Metal/Vulkan later, and old fixed-function/old DirectX deferred.

##### Topic 4 - Platform/provider architecture

The strongest synthesis was service-first/provider-backed/profile-selected/contract-governed/third-party-fenced. Services are Dominium-owned: platform, input, render, draw, audio, asset, script, UI, storage, diagnostics. Providers implement them: null, raylib, rlgl, rlsw, SDL2, raudio, Lua, later OpenGL33/D3D11/Metal/Vulkan/native host providers. Profiles select provider combinations. Third-party types must not cross into engine, game, contracts, content, saves, replays, packs, or public SDK.

##### Topic 5 - Language and compatibility baseline

The user stated that the project had transitioned to C17 and C++17 and raised minimum systems to Windows 7 SP1, Mac OS X 10.9.5, and Linux. The visible conclusion was to use C17 for ABI-facing law-like structures, packets, fixed-width types, deterministic math, save/replay records, and low-level facades; C++17 for runtime machinery, provider implementations, Workbench, apps, resource ownership, and orchestration. Public boundaries remain C-compatible and POD/versioned.

##### Topic 6 - Workbench as UI authoring environment

Workbench should be the tool that designs and edits rendered GUI, TUI, HUD, widget, theme, and workspace artifacts. It should edit Dominium documents and patches, not provider-specific widgets. It should support View Studio, Layout Studio, Control Catalog, Theme Lab, TUI Studio, Rendered GUI Studio, Preview Matrix, and Validation/Evidence panels. The first proof should not be a full interface editor; it should be a practical dashboard such as validation summary or node/body status shown across projections.

##### Topic 7 - Shared UI/UX system

CLI, TUI, rendered GUI, native GUI, headless, and immersive/VR should be projections of the same command/action/view/document system. The UI is not just buttons and panels; the reusable truth is commands, results, refusals, diagnostics, evidence, actions, views, documents, patches, and capabilities. This lets menus, setup, launcher, Workbench, server admin, client HUD, and robot OS shells share one architecture.

##### Topic 8 - Robotic seed-civilisation game design

The user explicitly defined the core game direction. Players are robots/machine consciousnesses seeded by a mothership. The game simulates energy, materials, machines, logistics, sensors, permissions, fields, construction, heat, maintenance, and machine graphs rather than human workers. The mothership gives knowledge and precision but not scale. Players build industrial chains, remote spawn labs, cities, and eventually terraforming/megaproject infrastructure. Nanotech is an actuator, not magic. Machine assemblies compile into functional process graphs.

##### Topic 9 - Planet generation, fields, and terraforming

The game should use star-system/planet generation, not initially a "galaxy generator." It should support forward generation from physical parameters and reverse generation from desired gameplay/planet characteristics. A common field-layer stack should support procedural generation, real data, overrides, mod packs, terraforming, construction, damage, and temporary previews. Gameplay layers must conserve matter/energy where appropriate; editor/worldgen layers need not.

##### Topic 10 - Robot OS, diegetics, and epistemics

The final UX direction is that the player boots into an OS, connects to nodes/universes, downloads consciousness into fabricated bodies, and operates through sensor/communication networks. The UI should reflect what the player's body, faction, node, or sensor network can know. Facts should have metadata such as source, confidence, observation tick, network path, latency, and permission. Fog-of-war becomes diegetic and anti-cheat relevant.

#### 4. What We Were Trying to Achieve

##### 4.1 Explicit Goals

The user explicitly wanted a clean, future-proof architecture for Dominium's GUI, binaries, platform support, renderer support, native apps, and directory structure. Later explicit goals included deep portability, modularity, extensibility, full replaceability of code/data/files/directories, reusable Domino engine code, a robot seed-civilisation game direction, raylib/SDL/Lua use as providers, and OS-like fully customizable UI/UX.

##### 4.2 Inferred Goals

INFERENCE: The user wants to turn Dominium into a platform-level project whose architecture can survive decades of refactors, ports, new games, new providers, and evolving standards. The user also wants future assistants to stop re-litigating already-settled principles and instead build upon the emerging doctrine.

##### 4.3 Goals That Changed Over Time

The GUI/binary goal started around native GUIs and compatibility lanes, then shifted to ownership-based repo convergence, then to renderer/provider matrices, then to Workbench/UI authoring, then to robot OS diegetics. The project language baseline changed from older C89/C++98 framing to C17/C++17. The game design changed from a broad MMO with invention/industry/logistics/trade to a more specific robotic seed-civilisation premise.

##### 4.4 Goals Still Unresolved

The exact first playable slice remains unresolved. The first provider wedge is clear, but exact sequence between client/workbench gameplay, Robot OS, and gameplay mechanics still needs a formal milestone. The exact Lua version, Linux baseline, current repo status, and external library/version support need verification. The exact degree of Unreal involvement remains unclear.

#### 5. Decisions Made and Why

##### Decision overview table

**Table rendered as row cards for appendix readability:**
- **Row 1:** **ID:** DECISION-01; **Decision:** Architectural direction is service-first, provider-backed, profile-selected, contract-governed, third-party-fenced.; **Status:** accepted by user / current doctrine; **Why it mattered:** Allows raylib/SDL/Lua acceleration without architecture lock-in.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 2:** **ID:** DECISION-02; **Decision:** Use C17 + C++17 as the mainline language baseline, not C89/C++98, pure C99, or pure C++11.; **Status:** accepted by user; **Why it mattered:** Modernizes implementation while preserving C-compatible boundaries.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 3:** **ID:** DECISION-03; **Decision:** Minimum first-wave systems are Windows 7 SP1+, Mac OS X 10.9.5+, and Linux, with 64-bit x86_64/ARM64 prioritized.; **Status:** accepted by user, external specifics unverified; **Why it mattered:** Defines what renderers, OS APIs, and toolchains matter first.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT/VERIFY
- **Row 4:** **ID:** DECISION-04; **Decision:** OpenGL 3.3 should be the first direct hardware-rendering target before Direct3D 11.; **Status:** accepted direction; **Why it mattered:** Keeps early rendering cross-platform and shader-first.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT/INFERENCE
- **Row 5:** **ID:** DECISION-05; **Decision:** OpenGL 1.1, OpenGL 2.1, and Direct3D 5/7/8/9 are deferred indefinitely/back-port research rather than first-wave targets.; **Status:** accepted by user; **Why it mattered:** Reduces architectural burden and lets project focus on shader graphics.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 6:** **ID:** DECISION-06; **Decision:** vector2d should not be a renderer backend; it should become a drawing/canvas feature layer.; **Status:** assistant recommendation apparently accepted by continued discussion; **Why it mattered:** Prevents backend taxonomy confusion.; **Confidence:** 3; **Label:** INFERENCE
- **Row 7:** **ID:** DECISION-07; **Decision:** Use raylib ecosystem heavily now as seed providers, not as architecture.; **Status:** accepted by user through subsequent synthesis; **Why it mattered:** Enables visible progress without letting third-party types become law.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT/INFERENCE
- **Row 8:** **ID:** DECISION-08; **Decision:** Keep Dominium apps generic and select provider combinations through profiles, not app-directory variants.; **Status:** accepted in refined synthesis; **Why it mattered:** Preserves modularity and replaceability.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** FACT/INFERENCE
- **Row 9:** **ID:** DECISION-09; **Decision:** Workbench should author Dominium UI documents, not raygui/raylib objects directly.; **Status:** accepted by sustained discussion; **Why it mattered:** Keeps UI customization reusable across CLI/TUI/rendered/native/immersive shells.; **Confidence:** 4; **Label:** INFERENCE
- **Row 10:** **ID:** DECISION-10; **Decision:** Dominium should become a robotic seed-civilisation game, not a labour-management game.; **Status:** explicit user statement; **Why it mattered:** Sets the gameplay, UI diegetics, fog-of-war, spawning, automation, and world simulation model.; **Confidence:** 5; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 11:** **ID:** DECISION-11; **Decision:** The UI should feel like a customizable robot operating system, with boot/node/body/HUD/workbench shells sharing code and artifacts.; **Status:** explicit user statement + accepted synthesis; **Why it mattered:** Unifies diegetics, menu/HUD continuity, Workbench, TUI/CLI/rendered/VR shells.; **Confidence:** 5; **Label:** FACT/INFERENCE
- **Row 12:** **ID:** DECISION-12; **Decision:** Setup/launcher should allow user choice only through verified compatible component profiles, not arbitrary unchecked binaries.; **Status:** accepted direction; **Why it mattered:** Supports customization without compatibility fraud or unsafe combinations.; **Confidence:** 3; **Label:** INFERENCE

##### Decision explanations

DECISION-01 through DECISION-12 above are the decision set that should carry forward. The strongest accepted formulation is service-first, provider-backed, profile-selected, contract-governed, and third-party-fenced. This decision matters because it reconciles the user's desire to use raylib/SDL/Lua now with the deeper requirement that Dominium remain portable and replaceable. It supersedes vendor-shaped architecture.

The language baseline decision matters because it changes what old renderers and toolchains matter. C17/C++17 reduces the need to design around C89/C++98 constraints but does not remove the need for C-compatible ABI boundaries. The system floor decision similarly moves many older renderers and OS targets into research/back-port categories.

The Robot OS and robotic seed-civilisation decisions are the most important design decisions. They are explicit user statements, not merely assistant proposals. They should be formalized as product/game requirements. They affect UI, fog-of-war, spawning, respawn, automation, industry, tutorial/planner design, and MMO anti-cheat.

#### 6. Ideas Considered but Rejected, Superseded, or Deprioritised

**Table rendered as row cards for appendix readability:**
- **Row 1:** **ID:** REJECTED-01; **Option:** One universal GUI framework defines the product family.; **Status:** rejected/superseded; **Reason:** Risk of framework capture and poor old-host/native fit.; **Final or tentative?:** likely final; **Reconsider conditions:** Reconsider only if project narrows to one platform/toolkit.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-05; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 2:** **ID:** REJECTED-02; **Option:** One GUI per OS version.; **Status:** rejected; **Reason:** Creates combinatorial explosion.; **Final or tentative?:** likely final; **Reconsider conditions:** Only reconsider for small high-value host lanes.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-05; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 3:** **ID:** REJECTED-03; **Option:** Content/domains as default destination for all domain material.; **Status:** rejected; **Reason:** Domain material includes code, schemas, registries, docs, packs, fixtures.; **Final or tentative?:** final; **Reconsider conditions:** Only use content for authored data/packs/assets.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-01; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 4:** **ID:** REJECTED-04; **Option:** vector2d as a render backend.; **Status:** superseded; **Reason:** It is drawing/canvas functionality, not backend equivalent to OpenGL/D3D.; **Final or tentative?:** final for first-wave; **Reconsider conditions:** Could exist as feature name, not renderer backend.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-04; **Label:** INFERENCE
- **Row 5:** **ID:** REJECTED-05; **Option:** OpenGL 1.1/2.1 and Direct3D 5/7/8/9 as first-wave renderers.; **Status:** deprioritized; **Reason:** New floors allow shader-first OpenGL 3.3 and D3D11.; **Final or tentative?:** temporary/back-port possible; **Reconsider conditions:** Reconsider for explicit old-host back-port.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-04; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 6:** **ID:** REJECTED-06; **Option:** raylib as the game architecture.; **Status:** rejected; **Reason:** Would contaminate core contracts/saves/replays/game law.; **Final or tentative?:** final; **Reconsider conditions:** raylib remains provider suite.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-02; **Label:** FACT/INFERENCE
- **Row 7:** **ID:** REJECTED-07; **Option:** Pure C99 or pure C++11 pivot.; **Status:** rejected; **Reason:** C17/C++17 gives better machinery while preserving C-compatible ABI.; **Final or tentative?:** likely final; **Reconsider conditions:** Reconsider only if host/toolchain constraints radically change.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-03; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 8:** **ID:** REJECTED-08; **Option:** Human worker/labour-management core game.; **Status:** rejected by user; **Reason:** User chose robotic seed civilisation; no human labour simulation.; **Final or tentative?:** final for current design; **Reconsider conditions:** Could appear only as later narrative/ecology layer, not core.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-07; **Label:** FACT
- **Row 9:** **ID:** REJECTED-09; **Option:** Workbench edits provider-specific UI objects as source of truth.; **Status:** rejected; **Reason:** Would lock UI to raygui/raylib/native toolkit.; **Final or tentative?:** final; **Reconsider conditions:** Workbench edits Dominium documents and projections.; **Related workstream:** WORKSTREAM-05; **Label:** INFERENCE

#### 7. Important Reasoning, Rationale, and Tradeoffs

The central tradeoff was speed versus purity. Raylib, SDL2, Lua, raygui, rlgl, rlsw, and related libraries can accelerate visible progress, but if they leak into game law, UI documents, saves, replays, or public contracts, they become architectural capture. The chosen compromise is to use them as providers behind Dominium-owned services and conformance tests.

A second tradeoff was breadth of compatibility versus first-wave progress. Earlier ambitions included very old Windows, old OpenGL, old DirectX, old macOS, 32-bit, and even exotic/retro systems. After the C17/C++17 and system-floor shift, these became back-port/research lanes. The project still preserves modularity for them, but it does not let them dictate the first renderer or UI architecture.

A third tradeoff was deep simulation versus playability. The seed-civilisation design can explode into full physics, ecology, climate, fluid, heat, economics, logistics, and AI. The visible rationale was to start with systems that produce gameplay leverage: field layers, cut/fill, material/energy-constrained construction, machine graphs, logistics, heat, maintenance, permissions, and fog-of-war. Full groundwater, ecology, dust storms, and atmospheric chemistry can come later.

A fourth tradeoff was customization versus trust. The user wants fully customizable UI/UX like an OS. That is compatible with MMO integrity only if the client can style, arrange, and summarize known information but cannot reveal hidden data, bypass permissions, hide critical warnings, or automate unauthorized actions.

#### 8. Plans, Future Work, and Next Steps

The immediate technical plan is PROVIDER-WEDGE-01: add provider manifest schema, third-party manifests, forbidden include validator, null providers, and raylib/rlgl/rlsw/raygui/raudio/asset providers. This produces a client window, Workbench viewport, simple drawing, audio smoke, asset preview, frame export, and ensures server/headless/replay still work without raylib.

The immediate design plan is ROBOT-OS-WEDGE-01: define command/action/view/document/patch/UI contracts and build one shared `view.node.status` projected as command output, text panel, rendered OS screen, and body HUD panel. This proves menu/HUD/Workbench continuity.

The immediate game-design plan is to formalize the robotic seed-civilisation doctrine into a spec chapter and choose a first gameplay loop, likely survey -> mine -> refine -> fabricate -> build simple power/logistics/spawn infrastructure.

Recommended next-action sequence:
1. Verify and pin external library/toolchain facts.
2. Create/update provider manifest and forbidden include validators.
3. Implement null providers and raylib provider wedge.
4. Clean component/render/provider matrices.
5. Build `view.node.status` as Robot OS proof.
6. Formalize game design pillars into a spec.
7. Design UI customization/security constraints.
8. Define first playable vertical slice.

##### 9.2 Inferred Constraints and Preferences

The user appears to prefer source layouts and naming that remain meaningful years later. They dislike vendor-shaped, framework-shaped, and status-word architecture. Future assistants should not propose "just use X framework" as the answer. They should instead map libraries to providers, profiles, and contracts.

##### 9.3 Uncertain or Unestablished Preferences

It remains uncertain how much the user wants Unreal in the near-term after raylib-first discussion. It also remains uncertain whether Lua 5.4 or 5.5 is preferred; the user appears to value pinning and stability more than "latest" for script ABI.

#### 14. What I Should Remember

- The strongest architecture phrase is: service-first, provider-backed, profile-selected, contract-governed, third-party-fenced, evidence-tested.
- Dominium should not become a raylib game; raylib is a seed provider suite.
- SDL2 remains a useful platform/input/audio provider.
- Lua should be pinned behind `dominium.script.v1`.
- C17/C++17 is the current mainline language baseline; C-compatible ABI boundaries still matter.
- OpenGL 3.3 first, D3D11 second, Metal/Vulkan later; old OpenGL/DirectX lanes are research/back-port.
- Workbench authors Dominium documents, views, widgets, layouts, themes, and workspaces, not provider-specific UI objects.
- The final game direction is robotic seed civilisation, not labour management.
- The UI should feel like a customizable robot OS whose HUD, menus, TUI, CLI, Workbench, and future VR shell are projections of the same system.
- The most important immediate next task is provider/Robot OS wedge planning with validators and manifests.

18. "Design the consciousness backup/disconnect/session model."
19. "Define protected alert classes that themes cannot hide."

#### 16. Compact Human Summary

This chat was one of the major convergence points for Dominium. It began from a transfer brief about GUI and binary strategy, but it grew into a broad architecture and design foundation for the whole project. The user's recurring concern was that Dominium must not become a one-off indie codebase or a pile of platform-specific UI and renderer hacks. The project should be portable, modular, extensible, replaceable, and reusable: not just for Dominium, but for other games on the Domino engine and possibly other future engine/game projects.

The early discussion preserved the GUI doctrine: every product should have CLI, TUI should exist or be stubbed deterministically, GUIs are optional/modular, and the product logic must remain behind stable backend contracts. Setup and launcher can use OS-native/OEM+ GUI shells; client is rendered-first; server is headless/CLI/TUI first; tools are case-by-case. This was tied to the repo structure problem: the source tree should be organized by architectural ownership, not by topics or historical accidents. The desired top-level roots are apps, engine, game, runtime, contracts, content, docs, tests, tools, release, archive, scripts, cmake, external, and later sdk/examples if needed.

The renderer discussion evolved significantly. Initially there were old lanes like OpenGL 1.x/2.x and Direct3D 9, but after the user stated that the project had moved to C17/C++17 and now targets Windows 7 SP1, Mac OS X 10.9.5, and Linux, the first-wave plan changed. Old fixed-function renderers and old DirectX should be deferred to research/back-port lanes. OpenGL 3.3 should be first because it supports early portable shader work across Windows/Mac/Linux, then Direct3D 11 for Windows, then Metal, Vulkan, and Direct3D 12 later. A key correction was that `vector2d` should not be a renderer backend; it is drawing/canvas functionality. Software rendering remains useful as a CPU framebuffer/reference/test/fallback path.

The strongest architecture synthesis was the service/provider/profile model. Dominium owns services and contracts: platform, input, render, draw, audio, asset, script, UI, storage, diagnostics, commands, actions, views, documents, patches, saves, replays, packs, profiles, and provider law. Third-party libraries implement providers. Raylib, rlgl, rlsw, raygui, raudio, rtextures, rmodels, SDL2, and Lua can be used aggressively, but only as replaceable provider implementations. Their types must not leak into engine, game, contracts, content, saves, replays, packs, game law, or public SDK. Apps remain generic; profiles select provider combinations.

The Workbench and UI discussion established that CLI, TUI, rendered GUI, native GUI, headless, and future immersive/VR interfaces should not be separate UI systems. They should be projections of one command/action/view/document model. Workbench becomes the authoring environment for UI artifacts: widgets, layouts, themes, TUI panels, rendered screens, HUDs, workspaces, preview fixtures, and UI packs. It edits Dominium documents and patches, then validates and previews them through providers.

The biggest design decision came near the end: Dominium should become a robotic seed-civilisation game, not a labour-management game. Players are robot avatars or machine consciousnesses. A mothership contains human knowledge, starter resources, spawn fabrication, and AI planning, but is limited by feedstock, energy, heat, rare materials, safety protocols, and throughput. Gameplay is exploration, surveying, mining, refining, fabrication, power, logistics, sensors, permissions, spawn labs, industrial cities, terraforming, and megaprojects. Nanotech is a construction actuator, not free matter or magic. Machines compile into process graphs. Fog-of-war is diegetic because robots only know what their sensors, satellites, relays, and faction networks know.

The final UI/UX synthesis connects directly to that premise. The interface should feel like a robot operating system. The player boots into an OS, connects to a universe, downloads consciousness into a fabricated robot body, and disconnects into backup/on-ice state. Menus, Workbench, HUD, CLI, TUI, GUI, and future VR shell are all projections of that OS. Users can customize themes, layouts, shells, HUDs, terminal styles, workspaces, and sensor overlays, but customization must not reveal hidden data, hide critical warnings, bypass permissions, or automate illegal actions.

The best next action is to implement the first provider and Robot OS wedges: provider manifest schema, forbidden include validator, null providers, raylib/rlgl/rlsw/raygui/raudio providers, client/workbench/server profiles, and one shared `view.node.status` projected into command, text, rendered OS screen, and HUD.



## Source 151: Reader Brief Dominium Architecture, Ui, Providers, And Robot Os Strategy

> [!SOURCE] `human_full_text` `conversation_advisory` Source: `docs/archive/conversations/architecture_ui_providers/dominium_architecture_ui_providers_robot_os_strategy__06_reader_brief.md`. Reason: reader-oriented conversation artifact.

### Reader Brief - Dominium Architecture, UI, Providers, and Robot OS Strategy

This chat is a major Dominium convergence chat. The top 20 things to know:

1. Dominium should be a long-lived platform, not a one-off game.
2. The core architecture is service-first, provider-backed, profile-selected, contract-governed, third-party-fenced.
3. C17/C++17 is the current mainline language baseline.
4. Public/stable boundaries should remain C-compatible, POD/versioned, and explicit.
5. First-wave floors are Windows 7 SP1+, Mac OS X 10.9.5+, and Linux, subject to verification.
6. Old renderers such as OpenGL 1.1/2.1 and Direct3D 9 are back-port/research, not first wave.
7. OpenGL 3.3 is first direct hardware renderer; Direct3D 11 follows; Metal/Vulkan later.
8. Software renderer remains important for fallback, CI, reference, and evidence.
9. `vector2d` is not a renderer backend; drawing/canvas is a feature layer.
10. raylib, rlgl, rlsw, raygui, raudio, SDL2, and Lua are providers, not architecture.
11. Third-party types must not leak into engine/game/contracts/content/saves/replays/packs/public SDK.
12. Apps stay generic; provider choices are profiles.
13. Workbench authors Dominium UI documents, not raygui or raylib objects.
14. CLI/TUI/rendered/native/immersive are projections of one command/action/view/document system.
15. Dominium should be a robotic seed-civilisation game.
16. Players are robots or machine consciousnesses, not human labour managers.
17. The mothership provides knowledge and precision but not infinite scale.
18. Nanotech is a construction actuator, not magic.
19. UI should feel like a customizable robot operating system.
20. Fog-of-war and UI epistemics are sensor/network/permission constrained.

Best next step: implement PROVIDER-WEDGE-01 and ROBOT-OS-WEDGE-01 specs.
